abstract: Modern LLMs scale at test-time, e.g. via repeated sampling, where inference cost grows with model size and the number of samples. This creates a trade-off that pretraining scaling laws, such as Chinchilla, do not address. We present Train-to-Test ($T^2$) scaling laws that jointly optimize model size, training tokens, and number of inference samples under fixed end-to-end budgets. $T^2$ modernizes pretraining scaling laws with pass@$k$ modeling used for test-time scaling, then jointly optimizes pretraining and test-time decisions. Forecasts from $T^2$ are robust over distinct modeling approaches: measuring joint scaling effect on the task loss and modeling impact on task accuracy. Across eight downstream tasks, we find that when accounting for inference cost, optimal pretraining decisions shift radically into the overtraining regime, well-outside of the range of standard pretraining scaling suites. We validate our results by pretraining heavily overtrained models in the optimal region that $T^2$ scaling forecasts, confirming their substantially stronger performance compared to pretraining scaling alone. Finally, as frontier LLMs are post-trained, we show that our findings survive the post-training stage, making $T^2$ scaling meaningful in modern deployments.

citations.fullCitation: Jordan Hoffmann, Sebastian Borgeaud, Arthur Mensch, Elena Buchatskaya, Trevor Cai, Eliza Rutherford, DDL Casas, Lisa Anne Hendricks, Johannes Welbl, Aidan Clark, et al. Training compute-optimal large language models. arXiv preprint arXiv:2203.15556, 10, 2022.
citations.justification: This paper introduced the 'Chinchilla' scaling laws, which are the primary point of comparison and the foundation that the main paper seeks to modernize. The T2 scaling laws are presented as a direct extension to Chinchilla, incorporating test-time inference costs that Hoffmann et al. did not consider.
citations.title: Training compute-optimal large language models

citations.fullCitation: Jared Kaplan, Sam McCandlish, Tom Henighan, Tom B Brown, Benjamin Chess, Rewon Child, Scott Gray, Alec Radford, Jeffrey Wu, and Dario Amodei. Scaling laws for neural language models. arXiv preprint arXiv:2001.08361, 2020.
citations.justification: This is the foundational paper that established the existence of predictable power-law scaling in language models, creating the research field that the main paper operates in. The concepts introduced here are the bedrock upon which Chinchilla and, subsequently, the T2 scaling laws are built.
citations.title: Scaling laws for neural language models

citations.fullCitation: Charlie Snell, Jaehoon Lee, Kelvin Xu, and Aviral Kumar. Scaling llm test-time compute optimally can be more effective than scaling model parameters. arXiv preprint arXiv:2408.03314, 2024.
citations.justification: This work provides the empirical motivation for the main paper's research by showing that increasing inference-time compute for smaller models can outperform larger models. The main paper formalizes this trade-off into a predictive scaling law that jointly optimizes training and inference.
citations.title: Scaling llm test-time compute optimally can be more effective than scaling model parameters

citations.fullCitation: Nikhil Sardana, Sasha Doubov, and Jonathan Frankle. Beyond chinchilla-optimal: Accounting for inference in language model scaling laws. In International Conference on Machine Learning, 2023. URL https://api.semanticscholar.org/CorpusID:266693796.
citations.justification: This is a direct and important predecessor that first attempted to integrate inference costs into pretraining scaling laws. The main paper explicitly builds on and extends this work by considering the more complex scenario of repeated sampling (pass@k) instead of just single-pass serving volume.
citations.title: Beyond chinchilla-optimal: Accounting for inference in language model scaling laws

intermediateReport:
## Detailed Report: Test-Time Scaling Makes Overtraining Compute-Optimal

This report analyzes the preprint "Test-Time Scaling Makes Overtraining Compute-Optimal," which proposes a novel framework for optimizing language model training and deployment.

---

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research was conducted by:
*   **Nicholas Roberts, Sungjun Cho, Zhiqi Gao, Tzu-Heng Huang, Albert Wu, Gabriel Orlanski, Avi Trost, Aws Albarghouthi, and Frederic Sala** from the University of Wisconsin-Madison.
*   **Kelly Buchanan** from Stanford University.

---

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

The development of large language models (LLMs) has been guided by two distinct lines of research: pretraining scaling laws and test-time scaling strategies. Pretraining scaling laws, exemplified by seminal works such as Kaplan et al. (2020) and Chinchilla (Hoffmann et al., 2022), focus on optimizing the allocation of pretraining compute between model size (N) and training data tokens (D) to minimize pretraining loss. These laws provide recipes for training compute-optimal models for a single-pass inference scenario. Conversely, test-time scaling laws (e.g., Snell et al., 2024; Brown et al., 2025) address how to efficiently allocate computational resources at deployment, often through techniques like repeated sampling (e.g., pass@k metrics) to improve model performance on downstream tasks.

Historically, these two research areas have evolved in isolation, leading to a disconnect in the overall optimization of LLM development and deployment. Pretraining decisions, such as model size and training duration, directly influence both the quality and per-sample cost of inference. Modern LLM deployments frequently involve test-time scaling, particularly repeated sampling, where inference costs scale with model size and the number of samples. This creates a trade-off that is not explicitly addressed by traditional pretraining scaling laws. For example, while Chinchilla recommends a ratio of approximately 20 training tokens per parameter, many contemporary LLM families (e.g., Llama-2-7B, Gemma-7B) are "overtrained," meaning smaller models are trained on significantly more tokens than prescribed by Chinchilla, primarily to reduce per-query inference costs at deployment. The existing frameworks lack a mechanism to connect these pretraining choices to the benefits and costs of such inference strategies.

Prior work has addressed isolated aspects of this problem. Sardana et al. (2023) extended Chinchilla to account for aggregate single-pass inference cost, but did not incorporate the multiplicative cost and performance gains from repeated sampling. Other studies empirically demonstrated that allocating more inference compute to smaller models via repeated sampling could match or exceed the performance of larger models (Brown et al., 2025; Snell et al., 2024), but these treated pretrained models as fixed entities rather than optimizing their initial training. Schaeffer et al. (2026) developed scaling laws to predict pass@k from pretraining compute, but approached this as a forecasting problem rather than an optimization problem for model training.

This work aims to bridge this gap by introducing "Train-to-Test (T2) scaling laws," which provide a unified framework for jointly optimizing model size, training tokens, and the number of inference samples under a single, end-to-end compute budget. This approach seeks to modernize existing pretraining scaling laws by integrating considerations for modern test-time deployment strategies, thereby providing a more comprehensive optimization framework for LLM development.

---

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The central objective of this research is to develop a unified scaling framework that jointly optimizes the computational resources allocated during both the pretraining and inference phases of a large language model's lifecycle. The authors identify a significant limitation in existing scaling laws: pretraining laws, such as Chinchilla, dictate optimal model size and training data quantity for a given pretraining budget, but they do not account for how the model will be deployed. Conversely, test-time scaling laws focus on optimizing inference-time computation, often through repeated sampling, but treat the pretrained model as a static input. This isolated development leads to suboptimal overall resource allocation.

The core motivation stems from the observation that modern LLMs are frequently deployed with strategies involving repeated sampling (e.g., generating multiple responses and selecting the best, or using techniques like self-consistency). In such scenarios, the inference cost grows with both the model size and the number of samples taken. This creates an unaddressed trade-off:
*   **Smaller models** are generally cheaper per inference sample but may exhibit lower per-sample quality.
*   **Larger models** offer higher per-sample quality but incur higher per-sample inference costs.
*   The benefit of repeated sampling is a non-linear function of the per-sample quality, meaning a slightly better base model can significantly reduce the number of samples needed, or vice-versa.

Current industry practice reflects an implicit understanding of this trade-off, with many smaller models being "overtrained" (i.e., trained on significantly more tokens than Chinchilla-optimal recommendations for their size) to improve their per-sample quality and reduce overall inference costs through efficient repeated sampling. However, there is no formal, principled framework to guide these overtraining decisions in conjunction with inference budgets.

The specific objectives of this work are articulated through three research questions (RQs):
1.  **RQ1: Should pretraining change if you know your test-time scaling budget?** This addresses whether an awareness of inference strategies should alter the optimal allocation of pretraining compute.
2.  **RQ2: Does T2 extrapolate to overtrained checkpoints?** This aims to validate if the proposed T2 scaling laws can accurately predict the performance of models trained in the overtraining regime they advocate.
3.  **RQ3: Does T2 scaling survive post-training?** This investigates whether the findings regarding optimal pretraining and inference resource allocation remain relevant after models undergo common post-training steps like fine-tuning.

By addressing these objectives, the research seeks to provide a comprehensive, end-to-end compute optimization strategy that bridges the gap between pretraining and test-time scaling, ultimately offering practical guidance for the efficient development and deployment of LLMs.

---

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The authors introduce Train-to-Test (T2) scaling laws, designed to jointly optimize model size (N), training tokens (D), and the number of inference samples (k) under a fixed total compute budget that includes both training and inference costs. The total training cost is approximated as C_train = 6ND FLOPs, and the inference cost as C_inf = 2Nk FLOPs (for k samples). The optimization problem is formulated as either minimizing a loss function L(N, D, k) or maximizing an accuracy function Acc(N, D, k) subject to these compute constraints.

Two complementary modeling approaches are developed for T2 scaling:

**Approach 1: T2 as a Parametric Model of the Task Loss**
This approach extends the Chinchilla scaling law, L(N, D) = E + A/N^α + B/D^β, by incorporating the effect of repeated sampling (k) on the task loss. To make repeated sampling compatible with the negative log-likelihood (NLL), the authors define an NLL-style objective based on the `pass@k` metric: E_i~D_task [-log(1 - (1 - p_i)^k)], where p_i is the per-sample success probability for problem i. Based on prior work suggesting that the negative log `pass@k` scales as a power law with k, the extended loss model is:
L(N, D, k) = E + A/N^α + B/D^β + G/k^γ.
In this model, E represents an irreducible loss floor, while the additional term G/k^γ accounts for the reduction in loss achieved through repeated sampling. This formulation ensures that for k=1, it reverts to the standard Chinchilla scaling law, and the irreducible loss E is approached as N, D, and k tend to infinity. The seven parameters (log A, log B, log E, α, β, log G, γ) are fitted empirically by minimizing the sum of squared errors between predicted and observed NLL values across a range of checkpoints and k values, using the L-BFGS-B algorithm with multiple random restarts.

**Approach 2: T2 as a Parametric Model of the Task Accuracy**
This approach directly models the `pass@k` accuracy as a function of N, D, and k, which is often more intuitive for practitioners. Instead of a naive approach that computes `1 - (1 - E[Acc(N,D)])^k` (which overestimates due to the concavity of `pass@k`), this method models the distribution of per-question single-pass accuracies. Following prior work, the per-question accuracy Acc(N, D) is modeled as a Beta distribution Beta(a_N,D, b_N,D). The parameters a_N,D and b_N,D are related to N and D via the Chinchilla loss estimate L(N,D) through a Beta regression model:
*   The mean `μ_N,D = a_N,D / (a_N,D + b_N,D)` is parameterized using a rescaled logit link on L(N,D).
*   The sample size `ν_N,D = a_N,D + b_N,D` is parameterized using a log link on L(N,D).
This yields specific formulations for `μ_N,D` and `ν_N,D` based on Chinchilla loss estimates and additional fitting parameters (θ0, θ1, θ2, θ3, θ4). The final `pass@k` model is then derived from the properties of the Beta distribution:
Acc(N, D, k) = 1 - B(a_N,D, b_N,D + k) / B(a_N,D, b_N,D), where B is the Beta function.
This approach is fitted in two stages: first, the Chinchilla scaling model L(N, D) is fitted to empirical NLL values; then, the five Beta regression parameters (θ0-θ4) are fitted by minimizing the sum of squared errors between predicted and empirical `pass@k` accuracy values.

**Inference Cost Correction**
To operationalize the joint optimization, the inference budget `C_inf` is fixed, and the number of samples `k` is made dependent on the model size N: `k = C_inf / (2N)`. This means smaller models are allocated more repeated samples for a given inference budget, reflecting a common practical strategy. This expression for `k` is then substituted into both the loss-based (Approach 1) and accuracy-based (Approach 2) T2 scaling models, allowing for joint optimization of N and D under combined training and inference compute budgets.

**Experimental Setup**
The models are evaluated on a testbed of over 100 checkpoints, including those from Porian et al. (2024) and additional "overtrained" checkpoints generated for this study. These models range from 5M to 901M parameters, trained on 50M to 120B tokens, and span 12 compute levels. Evaluation is performed across eight downstream tasks: four real-world benchmarks (LAMBADA OpenAI, ARC-Easy, SciQ, OpenBookQA) and four synthetic tasks (simple knowledge recall, multi-step arithmetic reasoning, commonsense causal reasoning, spatial reasoning). Macro-averaged results are presented unless specified.

---

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The research presents findings across three research questions, addressing the implications of T2 scaling for LLM training and deployment.

**RQ1: Should Pretraining Change if You Know Your Test-Time Scaling Budget?**
The study compares T2 scaling forecasts to traditional Chinchilla scaling, extrapolating predictions to 10^25 FLOPs. Using an inference budget `C_inf` equivalent to a single forward pass of a 70B Chinchilla model (140 B FLOPs), the findings indicate that pretraining decisions should indeed change. Both T2 approaches (loss-based and accuracy-based) forecast optimal models that are substantially smaller and more overtrained than those prescribed by Chinchilla. Specifically, the optimal "tokens per parameter" ratios are significantly higher than Chinchilla's recommended 20:1 rule of thumb. Approach 2 (accuracy-based) generally recommends more aggressive overtraining than Approach 1 (loss-based).

Analysis of isoFLOP curves demonstrates that at every compute scale, the optimal frontier for T2 approaches shifts considerably toward smaller, overtrained models that utilize more repeated samples, in contrast to the Chinchilla optimum. When inference cost is corrected, the Chinchilla optimal frontier exhibits non-monotonic improvement with increasing training compute (C_train), aligning with prior observations that smaller models with more test-time compute can sometimes outperform larger models. In contrast, T2 scaling, by jointly optimizing pretraining and test-time parameters, consistently shows monotonic improvement in performance with increasing C_train. These results collectively indicate that knowing the test-time scaling budget mandates a substantial shift toward overtraining during pretraining.

**RQ2: Does T2 Scaling Extrapolate to Overtrained Checkpoints?**
To validate the predictions, the authors fit T2 approaches to standard Chinchilla checkpoints and measured their extrapolation performance on 21 newly trained, overtrained checkpoints. The results show that both T2 approaches successfully extrapolate to the overtrained regime. Approach 1 exhibited a relative absolute error of 2.8%, while Approach 2 had a relative error of 8.4%, suggesting Approach 1 provided a more accurate extrapolation.

Empirical comparisons between the best overtrained checkpoints (predicted by T2) and Chinchilla optimal checkpoints at a fixed pretraining budget (C_train = 2.56 × 10^19 FLOPs) and inference budget (C_inf = 2 × 10^9 FLOPs) demonstrated consistent outperformance. Across all eight downstream tasks, the smaller, overtrained models consistently achieved higher `pass@k` accuracy than the Chinchilla optimal models. For example, on LAMBADA OpenAI, a 37M overtrained model achieved 49.90% accuracy, compared to 27.30% for a 455M Chinchilla optimal model. This empirically confirms that the overtraining phenomenon predicted by T2 scaling is not merely a model artifact but translates to improved performance in actual checkpoints.

**RQ3: Does T2 Scaling Survive Post-Training?**
The study investigated whether the T2 findings persist after models undergo post-training, specifically standard fine-tuning (FT) and supervised fine-tuning (SFT), on three real-world tasks (ARC-Easy, SciQ, OpenBookQA). Models were fine-tuned for 6 epochs.

The analysis revealed that the optimal frontier continues to shift toward smaller, overtrained models that leverage more test-time samples, across all three tasks and both fine-tuning methods. Both Approach 1 and Approach 2 showed consistent trends. The extent of optimal overtraining was found to be slightly "subdued" compared to the predictions for base models alone, a finding consistent with prior research suggesting that overtrained models can be more challenging to fine-tune. Nevertheless, this reduction was not sufficient to revert the optimal strategy back to the original Chinchilla recommendations.

Furthermore, empirical comparisons of the best overtrained FT and SFT checkpoints against Chinchilla optimal checkpoints confirmed that the overtrained models continued to outperform their Chinchilla-optimized counterparts after post-training. For instance, on SciQ with SFT, an 84M overtrained model achieved 66.80% accuracy, while a 347M Chinchilla optimal model reached 57.60%. Overall performance improved across the board compared to base models. These results indicate that the benefits of T2-guided overtraining are robust and persist even after post-training.

---

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

This work introduces Train-to-Test (T2) scaling laws, offering a framework that integrates pretraining and test-time compute considerations. The central finding is that when accounting for test-time compute via repeated sampling, the optimal pretraining decisions shift considerably towards training smaller models for longer periods, resulting in an "overtrained" state relative to traditional Chinchilla scaling recommendations. This outcome is consistently observed across two distinct modeling approaches (loss-based and accuracy-based), verified empirically with newly trained checkpoints, and demonstrated to persist after post-training procedures like fine-tuning.

The significance of these findings is primarily in providing a more comprehensive and practically relevant framework for large language model development. Traditional scaling laws, while foundational, do not fully capture the complexities of modern LLM deployment, where inference efficiency and performance through techniques like repeated sampling are critical. By explicitly modeling the interaction between training and inference costs and performance, T2 scaling offers a refined blueprint for resource allocation.

The potential impact includes:
*   **More efficient LLM development:** Developers can make more informed decisions about model size and training duration, leading to models that are optimized not just for pretraining loss but for end-to-end performance under realistic deployment constraints. This could result in cost savings by guiding the training of smaller, more performant models for specific inference budgets.
*   **Improved model deployment strategies:** The research validates the existing trend of overtraining smaller models for inference efficiency and provides a principled basis for it. This can inform strategies for allocating inference compute, suggesting that investing in more repeated samples for moderately smaller, overtrained models can yield superior results compared to using larger models with fewer samples.
*   **Bridging research gaps:** The work formally connects the previously disparate fields of pretraining scaling and test-time scaling, fostering a more holistic understanding of LLM compute optimization.
*   **Practical recommendations:** The authors provide a direct recommendation to practitioners: if the test-time scaling budget involves repeated sampling, training a smaller model for a longer duration is likely to be compute-optimal.

Future work suggested by the authors includes validating these overtraining recipes at larger scales, incorporating transformer-specific inference cost models for greater accuracy, and explicitly modeling the role of post-training within the T2 scaling framework to further refine the optimization process. This suggests the T2 framework could serve as a foundation for continued research into LLM life-cycle optimization.
overview:
## The Convergence of Pretraining and Test-Time Scaling

The development of large language models (LLMs) has historically been governed by two distinct sets of "scaling laws." On one side, pretraining scaling laws—most notably the Chinchilla laws—prescribe how to balance model size and the amount of training data to achieve the lowest possible loss for a given training budget. On the other side, test-time scaling strategies focus on how to allocate computational resources during deployment, often by generating multiple candidate responses and selecting the best one to improve performance on difficult tasks.

![Train-to-Test (T2) scaling framework](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.01411v1/x1.png)
*Figure 1: The Train-to-Test ($T^2$) scaling framework unifies pretraining compute (Chinchilla) and inference compute (Pass@k) to find a global optimum for model development.*

This research identifies a significant disconnect between these two phases. Traditional pretraining laws optimize for a "single-pass" scenario, where the model generates exactly one response. However, modern high-stakes applications frequently use repeated sampling, where the cost of inference scales linearly with both the model's size and the number of samples taken. This work introduces "Train-to-Test" ($T^2$) scaling laws, a unified framework that jointly optimizes model size $N$, training data $D$, and the number of inference samples $k$. The primary finding is that when a test-time budget is factored into the initial training plan, the "compute-optimal" strategy shifts significantly: it becomes more efficient to train smaller models for much longer than previously recommended—a state known as "overtraining."

## Bridging the Pretraining-Inference Gap

The motivation for this study stems from the practical reality of LLM deployment. Existing scaling laws like Chinchilla assume the goal is to minimize the final pretraining loss. This results in a "rule of thumb" where models are trained on roughly 20 tokens per parameter. However, many successful models in the industry, such as Llama-3 or Gemma, are trained on far more data than this ratio suggests. These models are deliberately overtrained to make them more capable for their size, thereby reducing the per-query cost during inference.

While practitioners intuitively understood this trade-off, there was no formal mathematical framework to quantify it. Specifically, the benefit of taking multiple samples—often measured by the $\text{pass@k}$ metric—is non-linear. A model that is slightly better on a per-sample basis might require significantly fewer samples to reach a target accuracy, or it might achieve a much higher accuracy for the same number of samples. By ignoring this interaction, traditional scaling laws provide an incomplete picture of the total cost of ownership for an AI system.

## The Train-to-Test ($T^2$) Framework

The authors propose two mathematical approaches to unify these costs. Both approaches assume a total compute budget that considers training cost $C_{\text{train}} = 6ND$ and inference cost $C_{\text{inf}} = 2Nk$.

### Approach 1: Parametric Model of Task Loss
This approach extends the standard Chinchilla power law. The original Chinchilla law is defined as:

$$L(N, D) = E + A N^{-\alpha} + B D^{-\beta}$$

Where $E$ is the irreducible loss, and the other terms represent the reducible loss as parameters $N$ and tokens $D$ increase. The $T^2$ version adds a third term to account for the number of samples $k$:

$$L(N, D, k) = E + A N^{-\alpha} + B D^{-\beta} + G k^{-\gamma}$$

To make this compatible with standard evaluation metrics, the authors define a negative log-likelihood (NLL) based on the probability of success. If $p_i$ is the probability the model solves problem $i$ in one try, the NLL for $\text{pass@k}$ is:

$$\mathbb{E}_{i \sim \mathcal{D}_{\text{task}}} [-\log(1 - (1 - p_i)^k)]$$

This formula captures how the "loss" on a task decreases as more samples are taken.

### Approach 2: Parametric Model of Task Accuracy
While Approach 1 is mathematically elegant, practitioners often care more about direct accuracy. However, simply using the average accuracy is misleading because $\text{pass@k}$ is a concave function—the benefit of more samples depends on how hard individual questions are. 

To solve this, the authors model the distribution of per-question accuracies using a Beta distribution, $\text{Beta}(a_{N,D}, b_{N,D})$. They then relate the parameters of this distribution to the Chinchilla loss. Specifically, they model the mean $\mu_{N,D}$ and the "sample size" $\nu_{N,D}$ (which controls the variance) as functions of the pretraining loss. The final accuracy for $k$ samples is then derived from the properties of the Beta function:

$$\text{Acc}(N, D, k) = 1 - \frac{B(a_{N,D}, b_{N,D} + k)}{B(a_{N,D}, b_{N,D})}$$

This allows the framework to predict exactly how much accuracy will improve if you change the model size or the number of samples.

## Why Overtraining Becomes the Optimal Strategy

The most significant finding of the $T^2$ laws is that the optimal "recipe" for a model changes when you know it will be used with repeated sampling. In the standard Chinchilla regime, the number of tokens per parameter stays relatively constant as you increase the compute budget. Under $T^2$ scaling, the optimal model size $N$ grows much more slowly, while the number of training tokens $D$ and the tokens-per-parameter ratio increase dramatically.

![Scaling optimal N and D](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.01411v1/x2.png)
*Figure 2: Optimal parameters ($N$), tokens ($D$), and the tokens-per-parameter ratio as a function of compute. $T^2$ models (red and blue) favor much smaller models trained on far more data than Chinchilla (black).*

The reason for this shift is the "inference correction." For a fixed inference budget $C_{\text{inf}}$, a smaller model can afford more samples $k$ because each sample is cheaper. Specifically, the relationship is $k = \frac{C_{\text{inf}}}{2N}$. When this is substituted into the $T^2$ equations, the math reveals that it is often better to have a smaller, "smarter" (overtrained) model that can take many shots at a problem, rather than a larger model that only gets one or two shots. 

For example, at high compute levels ($10^{25}$ FLOPs), Chinchilla might recommend a massive model. In contrast, $T^2$ scaling suggests that the optimal model might be orders of magnitude smaller but trained on an enormous amount of data to maximize its per-sample efficiency.

## Empirical Validation and Extrapolation

To ensure these mathematical models aren't just theoretical artifacts, the authors conducted extensive experiments. They utilized a testbed of over 100 model checkpoints, including 21 new "overtrained" checkpoints specifically created for this study. These models ranged from 5 million to 901 million parameters and were evaluated on eight diverse tasks, including reasoning, knowledge recall, and linguistic benchmarks.

The results confirmed that $T^2$ scaling laws accurately predict the performance of overtrained models. When the models were fitted on standard Chinchilla-style checkpoints and used to predict the performance of the new overtrained checkpoints, the error rate was remarkably low (2.8% for Approach 1 and 8.4% for Approach 2). 

![Extrapolation to overtrained checkpoints](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.01411v1/x4.png)
*Figure 3: $T^2$ scaling laws accurately predict the performance (NLL and Accuracy) of overtrained checkpoints (green dots) even when trained mostly on standard checkpoints (gray dots).*

In head-to-head comparisons at a fixed total budget, the $T^2$-optimal models consistently outperformed Chinchilla-optimal models. For instance, on the LAMBADA task, an overtrained 37M parameter model achieved nearly double the accuracy of a 455M parameter Chinchilla-optimal model when both were given the same inference budget.

## Stability Through Post-Training

A common question in scaling research is whether the patterns observed in base models hold after they undergo fine-tuning or other post-training refinements. To address this, the authors applied both standard fine-tuning (FT) and supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to three real-world benchmarks (ARC-Easy, SciQ, and OpenBookQA).

The findings showed that the $T^2$ scaling laws are resilient. While fine-tuning generally shifts the performance curves upward (improving accuracy for everyone), the *relative* benefit of overtraining remains the same. The optimal strategy still favors smaller, overtrained models that leverage repeated sampling. Interestingly, the degree of optimal overtraining was slightly less aggressive for fine-tuned models compared to base models, suggesting that while overtrained models are more efficient for inference, they may be slightly less flexible during the fine-tuning process. However, this effect was minor and did not change the overall conclusion: overtraining remains the compute-optimal path for systems using test-time scaling.

## Practical Guidance for Model Developers

This research provides a concrete roadmap for organizations developing LLMs. The traditional approach of training a model until it reaches a "compute-optimal" point on a loss curve is only optimal if that model is used for a single generation. If the intended use case involves techniques like self-consistency, majority voting, or best-of-$k$ sampling, the training strategy must change.

1.  **Plan for the inference budget:** Before starting pretraining, developers should estimate the expected inference load and the test-time scaling strategy.
2.  **Shift toward overtraining:** If repeated sampling is planned, resources should be shifted from making the model larger to training a smaller model on more data. This makes each inference step cheaper, allowing for more samples within the same budget.
3.  **Use $T^2$ to find the "sweet spot":** The provided formulas can help identify the exact parameter count and token count that will maximize accuracy for a given combined training and inference budget.

By unifying these two previously separate fields of research, the $T^2$ framework offers a more realistic and economically sound approach to scaling AI. It validates the current industry trend toward smaller, highly-capable models and provides the mathematical foundation needed to refine these strategies for the next generation of LLMs.

keyInsights: Optimal pretraining strategies fundamentally shift towards smaller, "overtrained" models when the total compute budget includes test-time scaling via repeated sampling.

The benefits of overtraining (relative to Chinchilla recommendations) are robust, translating to improved empirical performance in actual checkpoints and persisting even after post-training procedures like fine-tuning.
A unified framework for optimizing both training and inference compute is essential for efficient LLM development, as these phases are interdependent in modern deployment scenarios.
originalProblem: Traditional pretraining scaling laws (e.g., Chinchilla) optimize pretraining loss but do not account for modern test-time scaling strategies like repeated sampling, leading to suboptimal overall resource allocation.

Test-time scaling laws focus on optimizing inference compute but treat pretrained models as fixed, failing to connect pretraining choices to inference costs and performance gains from repeated sampling.

There is no formal framework to guide the industry practice of "overtraining" smaller models (training on significantly more tokens than Chinchilla-optimal) to reduce per-query inference costs when repeated sampling is used.
results: T2 scaling forecasts that optimal models at high compute scales are substantially smaller and more overtrained than Chinchilla-optimal models, with significantly higher tokens per parameter ratios, confirming pretraining should change.

Both T2 modeling approaches successfully extrapolate to the overtrained regime (with 2.8% and 8.4% relative absolute errors), and empirically, T2-predicted overtrained models consistently outperformed Chinchilla-optimal models on 8 downstream tasks.

The benefits of T2-guided overtraining persist after post-training (fine-tuning and SFT), with overtrained models continuing to outperform Chinchilla-optimized counterparts across tasks, despite a slightly "subdued" effect.
solution: Introduced "Train-to-Test (T2) scaling laws" to jointly optimize model size (N), training tokens (D), and inference samples (k) under a combined training and inference compute budget.

Developed two complementary T2 modeling approaches: one as a parametric model of task loss (extending Chinchilla loss with a k-term) and another as a parametric model of task accuracy (using a Beta distribution for pass@k).

Incorporated an inference cost correction where the number of samples k is made dependent on model size N (k = C_inf / (2N)), allowing for joint optimization of N and D under combined compute constraints.
summary.summary: Researchers at the University of Wisconsin-Madison and Stanford University introduced Train-to-Test (T2) scaling laws, a framework that jointly optimizes language model size, training tokens, and inference samples under a unified compute budget. Their findings indicate that accounting for test-time scaling through repeated sampling makes training smaller models for longer periods (overtraining) compute-optimal, leading to superior performance compared to traditional scaling recommendations.

title: Test-Time Scaling Makes Overtraining Compute-Optimal
